narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.6% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 29, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is much higher at 37.27, undergraduate GPAs average 3.24, and Year 1 and Year 3 deciles are typically 6 or above, placing your performance below those benchmarks. The three most influential factors in pushing the model toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your family income bracket. Your Year 3 decile of 2 had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signals performance well below what successful bar takers typically show in their later undergraduate years. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 3 reinforced this concern, indicating early academic challenges compared to passers' averages around 6. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, since while less common among bar passers (who are mostly middle to upper-middle income), it didn't weigh against you as heavily as your grades did. Being full-time, like 93% of those who passed, was neutral or supportive in the model's view. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic profile—consider retaking the LSAT to aim for 35+, building a stronger GPA through post-bac courses or targeted study, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps, so view this as a roadmap rather than a roadblock, and reapply after targeted improvements.
